title: About the Team
text: This project is developed by graduate students in the Energy Graduate Group at the UC Davis Energy and Efficiency Institute, under the supervision of Professor Alan Jenn.

title: About the Team
text: This project is developed by graduate students in the Energy Graduate Group at the UC Davis Energy and Efficiency Institute, under the supervision of Professor Alan Jenn.

title: About the Team
text:
The Team


Faculty Advisor


Alan Jenn, Ph.D.
Assistant Professor, Civil and Environmental Engineering Chair, Energy Graduate Group Institute of Transportation Studies
Research on plug-in electric vehicles: grid integration, technology adoption, ride-hailing electrification, and transportation finance. Developer of the Grid Optimized Operation Dispatch (GOOD) model. Ph.D. Engineering and Public Policy, Carnegie Mellon University. B.S. Engineering Physics, Math, and Astrophysics, UC Berkeley.
CEE EEI



Graduate Researcher


David MacDonald
Master’s Student, Energy Graduate Group
Remote and off-grid power, energy storage, microhydro systems, agrovoltaics, Smart Home technologies, repurposed electronics, passive energy systems, distributed energy infrastructure, environmental design, and energy resilience for emergency preparedness. Software engineer with Apple platform experience (HomeKit, CarPlay). Multiple provisional patents for low-energy mechanical systems.
EEI Profile



Graduate Researcher


Miguel Craven
Master’s Student, Energy Graduate Group Student Regent, UC Board of Regents
Renewable energy and biofuels, public policy, energy economics. B.S. Mechanical Engineering, UC Merced, where he served as Student Body President. Undergraduate work spanned electric vehicles, unmanned vehicles, medical assistance devices, and agricultural devices. Advocates for housing access, freedom of expression, and diversity in higher education.
EEI Regents



Graduate Researcher


Rayhan Khayrunnas Syarief Moo
Master’s Student, Energy Graduate Group Fulbright Scholar
Energy efficiency, renewable energy, geothermal, and energy policy. Engineering consultant based in Jakarta. B.S. Mechanical Engineering, Institut Teknologi Sepuluh Nopember, Surabaya. Pursuing graduate studies at UC Davis through the Fulbright Master’s Degree Scholarship.
EEI Profile

title: About the Team
text:
And One More Team Member…




Morty
Chief Morale Officer
Morty is the namesake of our sister project, MortyMonteCarlo, which computes the full lifecycle assessment (manufacturing, ingredient sourcing, packaging, retail) for the same dog food products simulated here. A key benefit of that project: we can now reverse-engineer any dog food recipe to its proper ratio and qualitative composition. He takes his role very seriously.

title: About the Team
text:
Acknowledgments

This work is supported by the UC Davis Energy Graduate Group and the Institute of Transportation Studies. The distributed compute infrastructure spans Google Cloud Platform, Microsoft Azure, and GitHub Codespaces. Route geometry is provided by the Google Routes API under academic licensing.
The simulation codebase and all artifacts are open source and available on GitHub.

title: Contribute Compute
text:
This research project needs Monte Carlo simulation runs across thousands of seeds and four scenario dimensions. You can contribute compute power using GitHub Codespaces — no local setup required.

Open a Codespace macOS Setup View Results

title: Contribute Compute
text:
What We Are Computing

Each simulation run models a refrigerated dog food freight shipment from a distribution facility to a retail store under realistic conditions:

4 scenario combinations: (dry vs refrigerated) x (diesel vs BEV powertrain)
Paired-origin design: centralized (Topeka, KS) vs regionalized (Ennis, TX) distribution
Stochastic traffic: time-of-day speed variation, incident delays, charging queues
Full cold-chain physics: TRU power draw, BEV charging stops, HOS rest breaks

Each worker runs n=200-500 Monte Carlo draws per scenario, producing 1,600-4,000 paired runs per batch. The functional unit is kg CO2e per 1,000 kcal delivered to retail.

title: Contribute Compute
text:
Codespace Quickstart

Time: ~5 minutes setup, then runs autonomously. Cost: Free with GitHub education or included Codespace hours.

Fork this repository to your GitHub account
Click Code > Codespaces > Create codespace on hotfix/derived-bootstrap-fix
Wait for the environment to build (~3-5 minutes)
Open a terminal and run:

N=200 SEED=$((RANDOM + 16000)) bash tools/codespace_run_production.sh
That’s it. The script validates the environment, then runs all 4 scenarios with your unique seed.
For maximum throughput (runs 2 scenarios in parallel):
export R_LIBS_USER="$HOME/.local/share/R/site-library"
mkdir -p sources/data/osm outputs
SEED=$((RANDOM + 16000))
STAMP="contribution_seed${SEED}_$(hostname -s)"

# Worker A: dry scenarios (background)
nohup bash -c "
  for PW in diesel bev; do
    Rscript tools/run_route_sim_mc.R --config test_kit.yaml \
      --scenario ANALYSIS_CORE --product_type dry --powertrain \$PW \
      --paired_origin_networks true --traffic_mode stochastic \
      --n 200 --seed $SEED --artifact_mode summary_only \
      --bundle_root outputs/run_bundle/${STAMP}/dry_\${PW} \
      --summary_out outputs/run_bundle/${STAMP}/dry_\${PW}/summary.csv \
      --runs_out outputs/run_bundle/${STAMP}/dry_\${PW}/runs.csv
  done
" > /tmp/worker_A.log 2>&1 &

# Worker B: refrigerated scenarios (background)
nohup bash -c "
  for PW in diesel bev; do
    Rscript tools/run_route_sim_mc.R --config test_kit.yaml \
      --scenario ANALYSIS_CORE --product_type refrigerated --powertrain \$PW \
      --paired_origin_networks true --traffic_mode stochastic \
      --n 200 --seed $SEED --artifact_mode summary_only \
      --bundle_root outputs/run_bundle/${STAMP}/refrigerated_\${PW} \
      --summary_out outputs/run_bundle/${STAMP}/refrigerated_\${PW}/summary.csv \
      --runs_out outputs/run_bundle/${STAMP}/refrigerated_\${PW}/runs.csv
  done
" > /tmp/worker_B.log 2>&1 &

echo "Running. Monitor: tail -f /tmp/worker_A.log"

title: Contribute Compute
text:
macOS Quickstart

Requirements: macOS with Homebrew (or willingness to install it). ~2 min setup if R is already installed.
git clone --branch hotfix/derived-bootstrap-fix --single-branch \
  https://github.com/dMac716/coldchain-freight-montecarlo.git ~/coldchain-repo
cd ~/coldchain-repo
N=200 SEED=$((RANDOM + 20000)) AUTO_RUN=true bash tools/bootstrap_macos_worker.sh
The script installs R (via Homebrew), required packages, validates the derived data, runs a smoke test, and launches production.
Monitor progress: tail -f /tmp/coldchain_worker.log

title: Contribute Compute
text:
How Results Are Used

Each run produces paired comparisons (centralized vs regionalized distribution) under Common Random Numbers (CRN), enabling statistically fair scenario deltas. Results are aggregated across all contributors to build convergent distributions of:

Transport CO2 emissions per functional unit
Trip duration and driver hours
BEV charging frequency and delay
Diesel refueling patterns
Cold-chain energy burden breakdown

These feed the graduate-level analysis documented in the Methodology page.

title: Contribute Compute
text:
Uploading Results

After your run completes, the results are in outputs/run_bundle/. To share them:
Option 1 — GitHub Issue (simplest): Tar the output and attach to an issue:
cd ~/coldchain-repo  # or /workspaces/coldchain-freight-montecarlo in Codespace
tar czf my_results.tar.gz outputs/run_bundle/
# Attach my_results.tar.gz to a GitHub issue on this repo
Option 2 — GCS upload (if you have gsutil configured):
GCS_BUCKET=coldchain-freight-sources N=200 SEED=20001 \
bash tools/worker_run_and_upload.sh

title: Cold-Chain Freight Monte Carlo
text:
Distributed Monte Carlo simulation of greenhouse gas emissions for refrigerated dog food freight under alternative spatial and powertrain scenarios.
Latest production audit: audit_2026-03-17 | 72,872 runs | TRAFFIC_AWARE_OPTIMAL routing Functional unit: kg CO2e / 1,000 kcal delivered to retail (audit-uniform method)

Methodology Route Map Transport Results Contribute Compute

title: Cold-Chain Freight Monte Carlo
text:
Study Design

This simulation compares the lifecycle transport emissions of two distribution strategies for dog food products under diesel and battery-electric (BEV) powertrains:



Dimension
Levels




Spatial
Centralized (Topeka, KS) vs Regionalized (Ennis, TX)


Powertrain
Diesel Cascadia vs BEV eCascadia


Product
Dry (kibble) vs Refrigerated (fresh/frozen)


Traffic
Stochastic (time-of-day variation, incidents)



Each paired draw uses Common Random Numbers (CRN) — exogenous uncertainty is sampled once per seed, then both spatial networks are evaluated with the same draw, enabling statistically fair delta estimation.

title: Cold-Chain Freight Monte Carlo
text:
Latest Results (March 2026 Production Audit)

The production audit aggregated 72,872 validated runs (61,118 diesel + 11,754 post-fix BEV) across 8 scenarios from 4 GCP and 4 Azure workers, with 100% functional-unit coverage using the audit-uniform method.
Headline finding: When route distance is controlled, the inferred climate advantage of BEV versus diesel depends strongly on the functional unit denominator. Under cube-limited physical loading (phase2 load model), BEV outperforms diesel by ~57%; under trailer-max normalization (audit-uniform), diesel appears to outperform BEV.

Denominator-free metrics: BEV emits 0.033 kg CO2/tonne-km vs diesel 0.051 (BEV wins by ~36%)
Per-mile: BEV 1.00 kg CO2/mi vs diesel 1.55 kg/mi (BEV wins by ~36%)
Audit-uniform FU: Dry BEV 0.028 vs diesel 0.020; Refrigerated BEV 0.046 vs diesel 0.015
BEV trip energy: 3,955–4,097 kWh propulsion, 15–17 charging stops per 1,743-mi corridor
Post-fix BEV runs correctly model en-route charging; all BEV runs have charge_stops > 0

This is a companion transport analysis to the full lifecycle assessment.



CO2 by scenario





FU Sensitivity: BEV vs diesel ranking depends on denominator method


See the full audit figures and FU sensitivity analysis on the Methodology and Transport Results pages.

Pipeline Overview



Simulation pipeline: 5 stages from data inputs through distributed compute to publication


See the Scripts Reference for detailed usage of each tool.

title: Cold-Chain Freight Monte Carlo
text:
Sister Project: Full LCA

This repository handles the transport stage of the lifecycle. The full lifecycle assessment (manufacturing, ingredient sourcing, packaging, retail) is computed in a companion project:
MortyMonteCarlo — Full LCA
That site mirrors and updates automatically when this repository publishes new artifacts to artifacts/analysis_final_*/. Both projects share the same functional unit (kg CO2e / 1,000 kcal delivered to retail) and product definitions.

title: Cold-Chain Freight Monte Carlo
text:
Download Results & Code

Download Submission Package (11 MB)
Includes: 72,872-row dataset, 20 figures, 10 tables, analysis code, simulation engine, tests, and documentation.
See also: GitHub Repository for the full codebase.

title: Cold-Chain Freight Monte Carlo
text:
Reproducibility


All pages consume committed artifacts — no live services queried during rendering
Every simulation run records model_version, inputs_hash, RNG seed, and timestamp
Route geometry uses Google Routes API with TRAFFIC_AWARE_OPTIMAL via direct shell curl
Derived data files are committed to the repository for offline reproducibility

Offline-first Artifact-driven Deterministic under fixed seed

title: Cold-Chain Freight Monte Carlo
text:
Quick Start
git clone https://github.com/dMac716/coldchain-freight-montecarlo.git
cd coldchain-freight-montecarlo
make test          # Run testthat suite
make smoke         # End-to-end smoke test

title: Methodology and Scientific Results
text:
This release documents the production audit audit_2026-03-17 aggregating 72,872 validated runs across 8 scenarios from multi-cloud workers (4 GCP + 4 Azure), with 100% functional-unit coverage using the audit-uniform method.

Scope: transport/distribution stage only
Functional unit: kg CO2e / 1000 kcal delivered to retail (audit-uniform method)
Design: matched-pair uncertainty within powertrain
Scenarios: BEV/diesel x dry/refrigerated x centralized/regionalized
Runs: 72,872 (61,118 diesel + 11,754 post-fix BEV)
FU coverage: 100%
Validation: PASS

Headline finding: The BEV-vs-diesel ranking reverses depending on which FU denominator is used. Under cube-limited loading BEV outperforms diesel by ~57%; under trailer-max normalization BEV appears worse. Denominator-free metrics (tonne-km, per-mile) consistently show BEV advantage of ~36%.
Previous baseline: local_chunked_run (80 runs, 2026-03-09) retained below for reference.

title: Methodology and Scientific Results
text:
This release documents the production audit audit_2026-03-17 aggregating 72,872 validated runs across 8 scenarios from multi-cloud workers (4 GCP + 4 Azure), with 100% functional-unit coverage using the audit-uniform method.

Scope: transport/distribution stage only
Functional unit: kg CO2e / 1000 kcal delivered to retail (audit-uniform method)
Design: matched-pair uncertainty within powertrain
Scenarios: BEV/diesel x dry/refrigerated x centralized/regionalized
Runs: 72,872 (61,118 diesel + 11,754 post-fix BEV)
FU coverage: 100%
Validation: PASS

Headline finding: The BEV-vs-diesel ranking reverses depending on which FU denominator is used. Under cube-limited loading BEV outperforms diesel by ~57%; under trailer-max normalization BEV appears worse. Denominator-free metrics (tonne-km, per-mile) consistently show BEV advantage of ~36%.
Previous baseline: local_chunked_run (80 runs, 2026-03-09) retained below for reference.

title: Methodology and Scientific Results
text:
Study Design

Scenario Structure

dry_diesel: Kansas origin, reefer OFF, diesel powertrain
refrigerated_diesel: Texas origin, reefer ON, diesel powertrain
dry_bev: Kansas origin, reefer OFF, BEV powertrain
refrigerated_bev: Texas origin, reefer ON, BEV powertrain



Paired-Uncertainty Strategy
Within each powertrain, dry/refrigerated rows share operating-condition draws (traffic, departure, ambient, stochastic delay). Paired deltas therefore isolate refrigeration + origin effects under matched operating environments.


Reporting Metric
Primary (audit-uniform method): Applied uniformly to all 72,872 rows:

payload_kg = payload_max_lb_draw * load_fraction * 0.453592
kcal_delivered = payload_kg * kcal_per_kg_product
co2_per_1000kcal = co2_kg_total / kcal_delivered * 1000

Denominator-free alternatives (ISO 14083-aligned, FU-insensitive):

co2_per_tonne_km = co2_kg_total / (payload_kg / 1000) / distance_km
co2_per_mile = co2_kg_total / distance_miles







Warning



FU sensitivity finding: Four candidate methods for computing the denominator produce divergent BEV-vs-diesel rankings. The audit-uniform method is the only one that covers all 72,872 rows with a consistent formula. See the FU Sensitivity Analysis for the full comparison.

title: Methodology and Scientific Results
text:
Input Data and Assumptions

Primary Inputs

Route/run outputs from outputs/distribution/local_chunked_run/phase2/*
Functional-unit transformed rows in transport_sim_rows.csv
Scenario and product assumptions from data/inputs_local/*.csv



Packaging Assumptions Used

Dry primary packaging: measured 0.1653 kg per 13.6 kg bag (0.0121 fraction)
Refrigerated primary packaging: measured mean 0.029 kg/package (27–31 g sensitivity)
Secondary refrigerated cardboard: treated as separate unmeasured term (not silently added)

title: Methodology and Scientific Results
text:
Validation and QA Gates
Fail-fast checks enforce:

scenario completeness (4 rows per replicate)
paired-uncertainty consistency within powertrain
dry reefer disabled (tru_kwh=0, tru_gal=0, refrigeration_runtime_hours=0)
route completion before FU export
finite positive total_kcal_delivered
finite co2_per_1000kcal
finite traffic metrics
plausible route distance/time

Validation artifact:

Validation report

title: Methodology and Scientific Results
text:
March 2026 Production Audit (72,872 runs)

The production audit (2026-03-17) aggregated 72,872 validated runs (61,118 diesel + 11,754 post-fix BEV) from 4 GCP and 4 Azure workers across all 8 scenario combinations. All rows have 100% FU coverage using the audit-uniform method (payload_kg = payload_max_lb_draw * load_fraction * 0.453592). A critical BEV charging fix was applied mid-campaign: pre-fix runs had 0 charging stops, post-fix runs correctly model en-route DCFC charging events.

BEV Charging Fix Context
The BEV charging continuation fix (PR #31) resolved three root causes that prevented en-route DCFC charging events from being modeled. Pre-fix BEV runs showed 0 charging stops; post-fix runs correctly model charging stops proportional to route distance.


BEV Charging Validation
The table below compares BEV runs before and after the charging fix, demonstrating that the fix produced the expected non-zero charging stops and associated energy/time impacts.

title: Methodology and Scientific Results
text:
Functional Unit Sensitivity Analysis

The choice of FU denominator is a first-order determinant of the BEV-vs-diesel comparison. Four methods were evaluated:

Phase2 load model — Cube+weight constrained packing (item to box to pallet to truck). Dry: ~16.4 tonnes, Refrigerated: ~3.4 tonnes. 80 validated rows.
Legacy stored — Original March 16 validated dataset (old pipeline). Unknown payload source. 46,720 rows.
Exogenous draw — Stochastic cargo weight from triangular distribution (8k–42k lb). Mean ~10.9 tonnes. 26,152 rows.
Audit uniform — Trailer max capacity x load fraction. Mean ~19.0 tonnes. All 72,872 rows.

Under cube-limited physical loading (phase2), refrigerated product fills only ~3.4 tonnes per truck due to case geometry. BEV’s lower absolute CO2 per trip gives it a ~57% advantage. Under trailer-max normalization (audit-uniform), payload is assumed at ~19 tonnes, inflating kcal_delivered by ~5.6x for refrigerated freight and compressing per-FU emissions — erasing the BEV advantage. This is an artifact of the denominator assumption, not a real change in energy efficiency.
Recommendation: Report denominator-free metrics (CO2/tonne-km, CO2/mile) alongside FU-based results. The FU sensitivity is itself a finding worth reporting.




FU sensitivity: two-panel comparison





Payload distributions by method

title: Methodology and Scientific Results
text:
Methods Reproduction
# 1) Merge run batches for reporting
Rscript tools/merge_mc_batches.R \
  --bundle_roots outputs/distribution/local_chunked_run/phase2/diesel,outputs/distribution/local_chunked_run/phase2/bev \
  --runs_out outputs/summaries/local_chunked_run_runs_merged.csv \
  --summary_out outputs/summaries/local_chunked_run_summary_merged.csv

# 2) Build presentation/scientific graphics + route animations
RUN_BEV_VALIDATION=false RUN_ADVANCED_DIAGNOSTICS=true RUN_SCIENTIFIC_GRAPHICS=true \
RUN_BEV_GROUPING_DIAGNOSTIC=false RUN_ROUTE_ANIMATION=true \
BUNDLE_ROOT=outputs/distribution/local_chunked_run/phase2 \
OUTDIR=outputs/presentation/transport_graphics_local_chunked_run \
RUNS_CSV=outputs/summaries/local_chunked_run_runs_merged.csv \
ANIM_OUTDIR=docs/assets/transport/animations/local_chunked_run \
bash tools/regenerate_transport_graphics.sh local_chunked_run

# 3) Render site
quarto render site/

title: Route Map: Factory to Retail
text:
Driving routes from both facilities to Petco Davis, CA. Blue: Topeka, KS (dry, 1,712 mi). Orange: Ennis, TX (refrigerated, 1,774 mi). Solid lines are primary routes; dashed are alternatives. Google Routes API, TRAFFIC_AWARE_OPTIMAL.



Interactive Map




Route Details
This map displays the actual Google Routes API driving routes from each distribution facility to the retail destination (Petco, Davis CA). Routes are computed with TRAFFIC_AWARE_OPTIMAL routing preference, providing both traffic-adjusted and static (free-flow) durations.

Blue routes: Topeka, KS (dry product facility) — 1,712 miles
Orange routes: Ennis, TX (refrigerated product facility) — 1,774 miles
Solid lines: Primary (rank 1) route; dashed: alternative routes
Click any route segment for distance, duration, and routing metadata

These routes are the geometric basis for the BEV charging waypoint plans and the Monte Carlo transport simulation.

title: Transport Monte Carlo Results
text:
Functional Unit Sensitivity Analysis






Important



Key finding: The BEV-vs-diesel ranking reverses depending on which functional unit denominator is used. This is a first-order determinant of the comparison for refrigerated cold-chain freight.


Four methods for computing the FU denominator (kcal delivered) were compared:









Method
Payload Source
Mean Payload (kg)
Coverage




Audit uniform
Trailer max x load fraction
~19,050
All 72,872 rows


Exogenous draw
Stochastic cargo weight
~10,882
26,152 rows


Legacy stored
Old pipeline (unknown)
~7,554
46,720 rows


Phase2 load model
Cube+weight packing
dry: 16,442 / refrig: 3,370
80 rows




BEV vs Diesel Ranking by FU Method














Product
Method
Diesel Mean
BEV Mean
BEV - Diesel
% Reduction
BEV Wins




dry
C_audit_uniform
0.0202
0.0284
0.0082
-40.4
FALSE


dry
B_exo_draw
0.0414
0.0531
0.0117
-28.3
FALSE


dry
D_phase2
0.0743
0.0325
-0.0418
56.3
TRUE


refrigerated
C_audit_uniform
0.0147
0.0463
0.0316
-215.6
FALSE


refrigerated
D_phase2
0.6684
0.2883
-0.3800
56.9
TRUE





Under cube-limited physical loading (phase2), refrigerated product loads only ~3.4 tonnes per truck. This small denominator amplifies per-FU emissions for both powertrains, but BEV’s lower absolute CO2 per trip gives it a ~57% advantage. Under trailer-max normalization (audit-uniform), payload is ~19 tonnes, inflating kcal_delivered by ~5.6x for refrigerated freight and erasing the BEV advantage — an artifact of the denominator assumption.



FU sensitivity: BEV vs diesel ranking by denominator method





Payload distribution by FU method

To avoid denominator dependence, ISO 14083-aligned metrics are also reported:

On a per-tonne-km basis, BEV (0.033) outperforms diesel (0.051) by ~36% for dry product, independent of the FU denominator choice. Per-mile, BEV (1.00 kg/mi) outperforms diesel (1.55 kg/mi) by ~36%.

title: Scripts Reference
text:
Script
Purpose
Usage




tools/run_route_sim_mc.R
Run paired MC transport simulation
Rscript tools/run_route_sim_mc.R --config test_kit.yaml --scenario ANALYSIS_CORE --product_type dry --powertrain bev --n 500 --seed 42


tools/run_route_sim.R
Single route simulation run
Rscript tools/run_route_sim.R --config test_kit.yaml


tools/run_chunk.R
Run a single Monte Carlo chunk
Rscript tools/run_chunk.R --scenario CENTRALIZED --n 5000 --seed 123 --mode SMOKE_LOCAL


tools/run_local.R
Convenience wrapper for local smoke runs
Rscript tools/run_local.R


tools/worker_run_and_upload.sh
Run sim, tar results, upload to GCS, clean
GCS_BUCKET=coldchain-freight-sources SEED=2001 N=20 bash tools/worker_run_and_upload.sh

title: Scripts Reference
text:
Route & Data Pipeline








Script
Purpose
Usage




tools/route_precompute_google.sh
Precompute facility-to-retail routes via Google Routes API
GOOGLE_MAPS_API_KEY=... bash tools/route_precompute_google.sh


tools/build_google_routes_cache_traffic.sh
Build traffic-aware OD distance cache
bash tools/build_google_routes_cache_traffic.sh


tools/run_google_routes_cache_pipeline.sh
Full OD cache pipeline (single entry point)
bash tools/run_google_routes_cache_pipeline.sh


tools/charging_stations_cache_google.R
Cache EV charging station locations along corridors
Rscript tools/charging_stations_cache_google.R


tools/route_precompute_bev_with_charging_google.R
Generate BEV charging waypoint plans
Rscript tools/route_precompute_bev_with_charging_google.R


tools/faf_extract_distances.R
Extract FAF5 freight distance distributions
Rscript tools/faf_extract_distances.R


tools/preflight.R
Validate inputs before simulation
Rscript tools/preflight.R --mode SMOKE_LOCAL --scenario CENTRALIZED

title: Scripts Reference
text:
Result Collection & Aggregation








Script
Purpose
Usage




tools/pull_worker_results.sh
Pull run bundles from all GCP + Azure workers
bash tools/pull_worker_results.sh --upload-gcs


tools/merge_run_bundles.sh
Merge and deduplicate summaries from all sources
bash tools/merge_run_bundles.sh --include-local --output-dir artifacts/analysis


tools/aggregate.R
Merge histogram chunks within a run group
Rscript tools/aggregate.R --run_group BASE --mode REAL_RUN


tools/get_codespace_results.sh
Poll Codespaces for completed run bundles
bash tools/get_codespace_results.sh


tools/submit_results.sh
Submit results as a GitHub PR
bash tools/submit_results.sh

title: Scripts Reference
text:
Analysis & Validation








Script
Purpose
Usage




tools/audit_analysis.R
Comprehensive R analysis with FU derivation, 10 figures, 5 tables
Rscript tools/audit_analysis.R <input.csv> <output_dir>


tools/validate_cross_platform_audit.R
Compare analysis across LOCAL/GCS/Azure for proof of computation
Rscript tools/validate_cross_platform_audit.R --local_csv data.csv --gcs_csv gcs.csv --outdir artifacts/validation


tools/gcs_full_audit.sh
Run full audit on a GCP VM from GCS tarballs
bash tools/gcs_full_audit.sh (run on VM)


tools/validate_artifact.R
Validate a single artifact bundle
Rscript tools/validate_artifact.R --artifact_dir outputs/run_bundle/foo


tools/validate_bev_plans.R
Validate BEV route plan coverage
Rscript tools/validate_bev_plans.R --bev_plans_csv data/derived/bev_route_plans.csv


tools/compute_fu.R
Single entry point for FU computation with method enforcement
Rscript tools/compute_fu.R --input data.csv.gz --output out.csv.gz --method audit_uniform


tools/fu_final_package.R
Publication FU sensitivity package (figures, frozen dataset, memo)
Rscript tools/fu_final_package.R


tools/fu_comparison_analysis.R
Generate 11 figures + 6 tables comparing all four FU methods
Rscript tools/fu_comparison_analysis.R


tools/recover_fu_backfill.R
Deterministic FU recovery for missing rows (sensitivity artifact)
Rscript tools/recover_fu_backfill.R


tools/build_audit_uniform_dataset.R
Build uniform audit-formula dataset (100% FU coverage)
Rscript tools/build_audit_uniform_dataset.R

title: Scripts Reference
text:
Graphics & Site








Script
Purpose
Usage




tools/regenerate_transport_graphics.sh
Full graphics pipeline (6 stages)
BUNDLE_ROOT=outputs/run_bundle/foo bash tools/regenerate_transport_graphics.sh run_id


tools/select_representative_runs.R
Pick median-CO2 runs for animation
Rscript tools/select_representative_runs.R --runs_csv runs.csv --out_csv rep.csv


tools/update_site_from_audit.sh
Update site assets from audit bundle, render, optionally commit
bash tools/update_site_from_audit.sh --audit-dir /tmp/audit --commit


scripts/render_run_graphs.R
Render diagnostic graphs for a single run
Rscript scripts/render_run_graphs.R --run_dir runs/<id>


tools/make_presentation_artifacts.R
Generate presentation-ready paired comparison artifacts
Rscript tools/make_presentation_artifacts.R

title: Scripts Reference
text:
Cloud Infrastructure








Script
Purpose
Usage




tools/bootstrap_worker.sh
Set up GCP or Azure VM (image-first, fallback to full install)
bash tools/bootstrap_worker.sh --platform gcp --name worker-new


tools/create_worker_image.sh
Create GCP VM disk image from configured worker
bash tools/create_worker_image.sh gcp-ta-worker-2 us-central1-a


tools/manage_worker_image.sh
Download/upload/deploy VM images
bash tools/manage_worker_image.sh download


tools/preflight_cloud.sh
Pre-flight check: quota, SSH, GCS, disk, running sims
bash tools/preflight_cloud.sh


tools/publish_run_to_gcp.sh
Upload run artifacts to GCS
bash tools/publish_run_to_gcp.sh

title: Scripts Reference
text:
Make Targets
make test                     # Full testthat suite
make smoke                    # End-to-end smoke test
make smoke-local              # Local-lane isolated smoke (n=50, seed=42)
make lint                     # lintr + shellcheck
make local                    # Quick SMOKE_LOCAL run (n=5000, seed=123)
make real SCENARIO=CENTRALIZED N=5000 SEED=123 RUN_GROUP=BASE
make aggregate RUN_GROUP=BASE MODE=REAL_RUN
make preflight MODE=SMOKE_LOCAL SCENARIO=CENTRALIZED
make route-chain              # Full route chain: routes -> stations -> BEV plans
make render-graphs RUN_DIR=runs/<id>
